₊˙♡﹗˚ ༘ req. by: takcyaki

u requested a neteyam ver, boo. i hope one of these two is too your liking. feel free to tell me if not or if there's anything else you'd like, i'd gladly do that for you!! much love xx

i honestly don't have a clear concept of how I'm arranging this as i don't want it to be gender nor sex based for inclusivity reasons. you can absolutely still do that in your personal chat as i am keeping it kind of open ended!! but in general i've got the same settings as for the ao'nung one:

actually did my homework for this one to figure out how to write it (🤓), and those are the things i learned/i'm basing this on:

• the req was for ao'nung to be in heat, but 'heat' actually refers to estrus, which is the phase in a female’s reproductive cycle where she's fertile and showing mating behavior. males don’t go into heat, but they often respond intensely to females in heat. some males like deer however do go into rut instead ☝️

• to stick w deers as examples, during the rut (which is during a mating season), males don't form permanent pairs with females. instead, dominant males (usually the strongest, most aggressive ones) gather and defend harems—groups of females. infact, i couldn't find any species that mates for life where the male would go into traditional rut. for those that do mate for life it more so seems to depend on abiotic factors and/or the females reproductive system

however,, animals that show the intense behaviour and reactions i frequently see in fiction like this are competitive in their mating behaviour, like e.g. many primate species where a male would take over an entire family if he was to succeed in fighting the other. but bc na'vi don't do things like that, i'd imagine their mating behaviour to be a lot more balanced and less offensive

i still didn't want this to be a na'vi mating season where everyone is getting it rn, so i gave the bot the following information about it:

•external stimuli like pheromones or temperature changes could trigger the na'vi's reproductive behaviors, making them more sensitive to hormonal changes in their mate and heightening the male's protective instincts

•while not exactly the traditional rut, the na'vi could have a mating season with optimal conditions for reproduction, involving rituals or behaviors where the male may show more dominant or protective actions

•the male might become more vigilant and territorial, ensuring other males or threats stay away, showing protective behaviors to keep
